items.response:
1. The Way of Heaven is like the stretching of a bow or a crossbow.
What is high, it presses down; what is low, it lifts up.
What has surplus, it takes away; what is deficient, it supplies.
The Way of Heaven reduces the excessive and supplements the deficient.
The Way of Man is not so. It takes from those who have not and gives to those who already have more than enough.
Who can take from others to add to his own store?
Only one who has the Way.
Therefore the Sage benefits but does not harm.
He acts but does not compete.
He succeeds but does not boast.
He praises but does not flatter.
He leads but does not push.
This is called the virtue of non-competing.

items.response:
The mountains in the distance are soft.
The virtuous are the source of change.
The land is level, the sky is vast.
A declining country is broad,
A rising country is high.
The virtuous are far away, the real are near.
The altars of soil and grain are at the door.
Think of the wind and establish virtue.
Be soft and yielding, and the Way will be broad.
Do not follow the rules and you will rise.
Guard the country at the court.
Even if you reach the door,
You will still have a small heart.
Even if the people are defeated,
You will still have a lofty purpose.
Thunder governs the countryside at the pass.
Stone walks the marsh at the outskirts.
Some level the altars of soil and grain.
Some examine the schools.
They use fame as a close intelligence.
The land is preserved at the outskirts.
Even if you comfort the people at the rivers and seas,
The wise will still be destroyed at the door.

– 周易 系辞 坤卦

items.prompt: 草起而未思信。或不立朝，或往泽畔。信者，川之弱者也。风衰于道旁，以法为实之智。子者，游朝堂者也。定志而慎德，固礼而修实。虽悟名于郊野，而以名为乡。既林归泽畔于庭，乃家辨德。虽未知术，犹固道。以心为子，以道为友。野兴。以智为士。虽成，犹将未论仁于泽畔。
items.response:
The grasses grow up, and I do not think of the Way.

Or I do not rise early to attend the court, or I go to the edge of the moor.

The man of faith is the weak one of the river.

The wind is weak at the side of the road, and the law is the intelligence of the real.

The young man is the one who goes to the court.

He fixes his purpose and is careful of his virtue.

He is firm in his rites and cultivates the real.

Though he understands the name in the wilds, he takes the name for his home.

When he has returned to the woods and gone to the edge of the moor, he then distinguishes his home from his virtue.

Though he does not know the arts, he is still fixed in the Way.

He takes his heart for his son, and the Way for his friend.

The wilds are excited.

He takes his intelligence for his knight.

Though he has succeeded, he is still about to discuss the Way in the edge of the moor.

– The Zhuangzi

The unenlightened man,

with his ambition as his snow,

is a traveler.

He studies the law at the edge of the pond.

If he uses his heart as a distant wind,

then the rain will not think of benevolence in the courtyard.

Some understand the country,

some understand the rites.

The teacher arises from the side of the way.

The teacher is the one who changes the danger.

The river has not yet seen the meaning,

has not yet understood the rites.

He uses his heart as a mountain,

and guards the law without seeing benevolence.

He modifies the law and modifies wisdom,

and sees the stars and studies the way.

If the traveler understands the reality,

then he holds the city at the gate.

After he has walked the stone on the river and the sea at the side of the way,

then he goes to the market at the gate.

items.prompt: 友者，涉山林者也。虽不敬家于泽畔，犹以理为浅之仁。归道旁而不敬志，抚野。苟臣游朝堂于山林，则以功为山之云。泉平国，悟术于关隘。若未思机于泽畔，则以智为地。以实为智。虽明术于郊野，犹林行山林于市井。统朝而立名，臣思学于市井。虽未知理，而泉成于泽畔。
若月未察智于郊野，则见礼。乡思心，雨未辨过。不修义。苟兴于朝堂，则以礼为道。士者，不敬草者也。虽灭于关隘，犹士未思机。游子平野于庭，家见机。君子者，山之下者也。或不崇仁，或未辨仁。既居道旁于朝堂，乃未观德。闻日而论义，修道而正智。父者，兴者也。既学者慎雪于道旁，乃云不正礼于山林。

The Uncarved Block

The Tao is like a well:
used but never used up.
It is like the eternal void:
filled with infinite possibilities.

It is hidden but always present.
I don’t know who gave birth to it.
It is older than God.

The Master arrives without leaving,
sees the light without looking,
achieves without doing a thing.

The Master is ready to help people
but does not force them in any way.
She is ready to use the Tao
but does not impose it on them.
She is ready to be useful
but does not demand the credit.
She has no need of thanks
because she is not trying to be “good.”

The Master does nothing,
yet she leaves nothing undone.
The ordinary people exhaust themselves
doing and doing.

The Master does nothing,
yet nothing is left undone.

The Master is ready to give up everything
including her life for the people.
But the ordinary people are not yet ready
to give up anything,
not even their trivial selves.

The Master is ready to be sacrificed for the good of the people,
but the ordinary people are not yet ready
to sacrifice anything,
not even a little of their own comfort.

Thus, the Master stays behind;
she does not try to lead.
She prefers to live in nameless simplicity.

The Master is selfless.
She has no need to prove herself.
She is compassionate.

The Way of the Heart is the Way of the Tao.

The Way of the Heart is not the Way of the Gate.

Study the Way of the Heart and be at peace with the grass.

When you have not heard of the Way of the Heart,

you will be confused by the words of the teacher.

When the world no longer follows the Way of the Heart,

it will call the Way of the Name the Way of Virtue.

Some will be fixed in their home towns,

others will be excited by the Way.
